questions.text: Which of the following is an effective technique for identifying conceptual classes in the domain model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Listing nouns and noun phrases from use case descriptions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Consulting a category list of common domain objects (e.g., places, roles, transactions).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reusing existing data models from similar systems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All of the above.

questions.explanation: Domain class identification typically involves linguistic analysis (noun extraction), category-based checklists, and historical knowledge from previous systems in the same domain.

questions.text: During the Object-Oriented Analysis phase, is it standard practice for a developer to define operation contracts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: True

choices.label: B
choices.text: False

questions.explanation: Operation contracts are part of the analysis phase; they formalize what system operations do (pre/post-conditions) based on the use case requirements.

questions.text: Software frameworks often incorporate multiple design patterns to provide extensible skeletons for applications.

choices.label: A
choices.text: True

choices.label: B
choices.text: False

questions.explanation: Frameworks leverage patterns like the Template Method or Strategy to allow developers to hook into existing code and customize behavior.

questions.text: In a high-level Domain Model, it is recommended to include method signatures and visibility (public/private) for all classes.

choices.label: A
choices.text: True

choices.label: B
choices.text: False

questions.explanation: A conceptual domain model should focus on real-world entities and associations. Detailed software artifacts like method signatures and visibility are part of the Design Class Diagram (DCD).

questions.text: Brownfield engineering is defined as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Developing a completely new system with no prior constraints.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Developing or evolving a system within the context of existing legacy software and infrastructure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Designing a system using only open-source tools.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decommissioning an old software system.

questions.explanation: Unlike Greenfield engineering, Brownfield engineering involves building upon or integrating with existing 'contaminated' or established legacy systems.

questions.text: The use of 'is-a' relationships in a system description (e.g., 'A Manager is a type of Employee') typically helps identify:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aggregation and Composition

choices.label: B
choices.text: Attributes and Types

choices.label: C
choices.text: Generalization and Specialization (Inheritance)

choices.label: D
choices.text: System Operations

questions.explanation: 'Is-a' statements indicate a hierarchy where a subclass specializes a superclass, mapped to Generalization in UML.

questions.text:
Based on the provided use case diagram for the **Library Management System (LMS)**, what is the post-condition for the use case "Borrow Book"?

```mermaid
graph LR
    Member((Member))
    subgraph "Library Management System (LMS)"
        Overdue([Fine Calculated])
        Borrow([Borrow Book])
        UC_Search([Search Catalog])
        Login([Login])
        UC_Update([Update Inventory])

        Member --- Borrow
        Member --- UC_Search

        Borrow -. "<<include>>" .-> Login
        Borrow -. "<<include>>" .-> UC_Update

        Overdue -. "<<extend>>" .-> Borrow
        UC_Search -. "<<include>>" .-> Login
    end
```

choices.label: A
choices.text: The book record is updated to reflect the new loan status.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The member provides their library card.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The member must have no outstanding fines.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The book must be available in the stacks.

questions.explanation: A post-condition describes the state of the system after completion. Updating the inventory/loan status is the result of borrowing a book.

questions.text: What is the relationship between the use case "Search Catalog" and the use case "Login" in the LMS diagram?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relationship "include"; base case is "Login", included case is "Search Catalog".

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relationship "include"; base case is "Search Catalog", included case is "Login".

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relationship "extend"; base case is "Login", extending case is "Search Catalog".

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relationship "extend"; base case is "Search Catalog", extending case is "Login".

questions.explanation: The arrow points from Search Catalog to Login, indicating that Search Catalog depends on Login as a mandatory inclusion.

questions.text: Does the diagram correctly represent that every time a "Borrow Book" operation occurs, the "Update Inventory" use case must also execute?

choices.label: A
choices.text: True

choices.label: B
choices.text: False

questions.explanation: The `<<include>>` relationship indicates that the behavior of the included use case is a required part of the base use case's flow.

questions.text: How many total included use case(s) are triggered by the "Borrow Book" use case?
questions.explanation: The diagram shows two dashed arrows with `<<include>>` originating from "Borrow Book": one to "Login" and one to "Update Inventory".

questions.text: What is a likely entry condition (pre-condition) for the use case "Borrow Book"?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The member must have a valid membership account.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The system prints a receipt.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The librarian puts the book back on the shelf.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The system sends an email reminder.

questions.explanation: Pre-conditions must be true before a use case can start. Having a valid account is a prerequisite for borrowing.

questions.text: What is the relationship between "Fine Calculated" and "Borrow Book"?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relationship is «extend»; "Borrow Book" is the base case.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relationship is «extend»; "Fine Calculated" is the base case.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relationship is «include»; "Borrow Book" is the base case.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relationship is «include»; "Fine Calculated" is the base case.

questions.explanation: The «extend» relationship is used for optional or conditional behavior. Calculating a fine only happens under specific conditions (e.g., if there are overdue items), making it an extension of the base Borrow Book process.

questions.text: In the LMS diagram, which use cases does the actor "Member" interact with directly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Login and Update Inventory

choices.label: B
choices.text: Borrow Book and Search Catalog

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fine Calculated only

choices.label: D
choices.text: All use cases inside the system boundary

questions.explanation: Solid lines connect the Actor (Member) to "Borrow Book" and "Search Catalog". Other use cases are reached through internal relationships (include/extend).

questions.text: What is the most likely final step in the basic flow for "Borrow Book"?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The system displays the book's due date to the member.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The member searches for a book by title.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The member enters their password incorrectly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The librarian logs into the system admin panel.

questions.explanation: The final step of the 'happy path' (basic flow) is the system providing the final output or confirmation of the successful transaction.

questions.text: In a UML Domain Model, the term **multiplicity** refers to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of methods in a class.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of instances of one class that can be associated with one instance of another class.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of subclasses an entity has.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of developers working on a specific module.

questions.explanation: Multiplicity (e.g., 1..*, 0..1) defines how many objects participate in a relationship.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of a System Sequence Diagram (SSD) in the analysis phase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To show the internal logic and method calls within the code.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To illustrate the physical deployment of servers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To visualize the interactions between external actors and the system as a black box.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To define the database schema.

questions.explanation: An SSD treats the system as a single object (black box) and shows the sequence of system events generated by actors.

questions.text: Which UML relationship describes a 'part-of' relationship where the 'part' cannot exist without the 'whole' (strong ownership)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aggregation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Generalization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Composition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Realization

questions.explanation: Composition (filled diamond) implies that the lifecycle of the part is managed by the whole; if the whole is destroyed, the parts are destroyed.

questions.text: An **Association Class** is used when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A class has too many attributes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An attribute belongs to the relationship between two classes rather than to either class individually.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A class inherits from multiple parents.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no relationship between two entities.

questions.explanation: If information (like 'grade' in a relationship between 'Student' and 'Course') belongs to the link itself, an Association Class is the correct UML representation.

questions.text: In UML Use Case modeling, an **Actor** can only represent a human user.

choices.label: A
choices.text: True

choices.label: B
choices.text: False

questions.explanation: An actor represents a role played by an external entity, which could be a human, another software system, or a hardware device.

questions.text: Object-Oriented Design (OOD) differs from Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA) primarily because OOD focuses on:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Understanding the problem domain.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defining the software solution and its technical implementation details.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Interviewing stakeholders to gather requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Creating use case diagrams for the first time.

questions.explanation: While OOA focuses on 'what' the system should do (domain concepts), OOD focuses on 'how' it will be built in software (design patterns, class signatures, UI, etc.).
